title: About
text:
This site is a personal data journalism project focused on college and
      professional basketball. Every post is built from raw game-by-game data —
      no box-score summaries, no recycled narratives. Just the data, visualized
      and interpreted.
    
    
      A basketball fan who believes the best stories in sports are hiding in the
      data. Built this site to share those stories publicly.

title: Classroom to Court
text:
Basketball has always been more than a game — it’s a laboratory of strategy, athleticism, and storytelling. Classroom to Court bridges the gap between the academic study of sport and the hardwood itself, turning raw statistics into narratives that every fan can appreciate.
Whether you follow the high-stakes drama of March Madness or track every NBA standings shift across an 82-game season, this site gives you something most coverage doesn’t: the numbers behind the moments. Every chart, every breakdown, and every post here is built from real game-by-game data — no filler, no recycled takes, just basketball and the statistics that explain it.

text:
Advantage Summary

adv_df <- data.frame(
  Category  = c(pts_label, opp_label, diff_label, "Win %"),
  Team_1    = c(s1$pts, s1$opp_pts, s1$pt_diff, s1$win_pct),
  Team_2    = c(s2$pts, s2$opp_pts, s2$pt_diff, s2$win_pct),
  Lower_Better = c(FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)
) %>%
  mutate(
    Advantage = case_when(
      !Lower_Better & Team_1 > Team_2 ~ team_1,
      !Lower_Better & Team_2 > Team_1 ~ team_2,
      Lower_Better  & Team_1 < Team_2 ~ team_1,
      Lower_Better  & Team_2 < Team_1 ~ team_2,
      TRUE ~ "Even"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(Category, Advantage)

t1_adv <- sum(adv_df$Advantage == team_1)
t2_adv <- sum(adv_df$Advantage == team_2)

title: NBA Player Season Grade Report
text:
How the Grade Is Calculated
Each category is graded against NBA starter-level averages from the 2024-25 season and converted to a 4.0 scale. Scoring carries the most weight (22%), followed by field goal percentage (12%), rebounding (15%), and assists (13%). Rebounding and assist benchmarks shift automatically based on the position set at the top of the file — a center is held to a higher rebounding standard than a guard.
The Efficiency Index adds together points, rebounds, assists, steals, and blocks, then subtracts turnovers and missed field goals — a simple measure of overall contribution per game.
The shooting splits chart compares the player’s percentages directly against the NBA average so you can see at a glance where they over- or under-perform.

text:
Scoring Trend

trend_data <- team_data %>%
  mutate(
    game_date  = as.Date(game_date),
    team_score = as.numeric(team_score),
    opp_score  = as.numeric(opponent_team_score),
    result     = ifelse(team_winner == TRUE, "Win", "Loss")
  ) %>%
  arrange(game_date) %>%
  mutate(game_num = row_number())

ggplot(trend_data, aes(x = game_num)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = team_score), color = "#2ecc71", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = opp_score),  color = "#e74c3c", linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +

  scale_color_manual(values = c("Win"="#2ecc71","Loss"="#e74c3c"), name = "Result") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = avg$pts, linetype = "dotted", color = "#2ecc71", linewidth = 0.7) +

    title    = paste(team_name_full, "— Scoring by Game"),
    subtitle = "Green = team score  |  Red dashed = opponent score  |  Dotted = season average",

    y        = "Points"

title: NBA Team Season Grade Report
text:
Win / Loss Rolling Record

trend_data <- trend_data %>%
  mutate(win_flag = ifelse(result == "Win", 1, 0),
         cumulative_wins   = cumsum(win_flag),
         cumulative_losses = cumsum(1 - win_flag))

trend_long <- trend_data %>%
  select(game_num, cumulative_wins, cumulative_losses) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(cumulative_wins, cumulative_losses),
               names_to = "Type", values_to = "Count") %>%
  mutate(Type = recode(Type, cumulative_wins="Wins", cumulative_losses="Losses"))

ggplot(trend_long, aes(x = game_num, y = Count, color = Type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Wins"="#2ecc71","Losses"="#e74c3c")) +
  labs(
    title    = paste(team_name_full, "— Cumulative Win/Loss Record"),
    x        = "Game Number",
    y        = "Games",
    color    = NULL

  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +

title: Michigan State: Win Prediction Model
text:
Michigan State vs. Michigan men’s basketball, in-game action. Photo by Michael Barera, CC BY-SA 4.0.



library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(plotly)
library(gtExtras)

msu_basketball <- read_csv("../../../pipeline/ncaa/basketball/data/team_stats/team_Michigan_State_2026.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)


msu_basketball |>
  ggplot(aes(field_goal_pct, as.numeric(team_winner)))+
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = .5)+
  geom_smooth(method = "glm", method.args = list(family = "binomial"), se = FALSE)+
  theme_bw()+
  labs(title = "Team winner explained by field goal percentage",
       x = "Field goal percentage",
       y = "Team Wins")









Interpretation - The graph indicates the higher the field goal percentage in a game is a good predictor of whether or not the team wins the game or not


I would like to create a simple logistic regression model for Michigan State basketball.
A single numeric predictor- Is there a meaningful way to predict whether Michigan State basketball wins or not? The outcome variable is team_winner(win/loss) and the predictor variable is field goal percentage.
Interpretation of logistic model
The S-curve is doing exactly what it should. Below about FG% = 35, the curve sits near 0 — Michigan State essentially never wins games where they shoot that poorly. Above about FG% = 55, the curve is pinned near 1 — they essentially always win. In between, the curve rises steeply, which visually confirms the strong effect size your model found (odds ratio ≈ 1.49 per point of FG%).


Logistic regression model. A simple model with one numeric variable as a predictor variable

win_model <- glm(team_winner ~ field_goal_pct, data = msu_basketball, family = "binomial")

summary(win_model)


Call:
glm(formula = team_winner ~ field_goal_pct, family = "binomial", 
    data = msu_basketball)

Coefficients:
               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)    -16.5225     6.2638  -2.638  0.00835 **
field_goal_pct   0.3977     0.1433   2.776  0.00550 **
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 37.628  on 34  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 19.120  on 33  degrees of freedom
AIC: 23.12

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6




Interpretation of a generalized logistic model.
The natural logarithm of the odds is (0.3977) which is positive so the probability is increasing. It tells us the direction of travel. As field_goal_percentage increases the chances of winning increase, due to the positive score. The p value is (0.00550) which is very small. This is a statistically significant result. This model tell us that field goal percentage has a positive relationship with winning games.
Because team_winner is binary, this model estimates the log-odds of winning as a function of field goal percentage — a positive field_goal_pct coefficient means higher shooting percentages are associated with a higher probability of winning. Exponentiating the coefficient (exp(coef(win_model))) converts that into an odds ratio: for example, a value of 1.15 would mean each one-point increase in field goal percentage multiplies the odds of winning by 1.15, holding nothing else constant.
Bottom line for your write-up: there is a statistically significant, strong positive relationship between field goal percentage and win probability for Michigan State this season — each additional point of FG% is associated with roughly a 49% increase in the odds of winning.
Field goal percentage matters, and matters a lot statistically. The coefficient for field_goal_pct is 0.3977 with p = 0.0055 — well under the standard 0.05 threshold, so there’s strong evidence of a real relationship, not just noise in this sample.
Odds ratio (that last output, exp(coef)): 1.488458 for field_goal_pct. That means for every 1-percentage-point increase in field goal percentage, the odds of Michigan State winning multiply by about 1.49 — roughly a 49% increase in the odds of winning per point of FG%. That compounds fast: a 10-point jump in FG% would multiply the odds by 1.49^10 ≈ 57x.


Multiple logistic regression. I want to determine if field goal percentage and turnovers influence whether or not the team wins. Common sense says that it plays a huge part.

win_model_multi <- glm(
  team_winner ~ field_goal_pct + turnovers,
  data = msu_basketball,
  family = "binomial"
)

summary(win_model_multi)


Call:
glm(formula = team_winner ~ field_goal_pct + turnovers, family = "binomial", 
    data = msu_basketball)

Coefficients:
               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)    -18.8593     7.5493  -2.498  0.01248 * 
field_goal_pct   0.4163     0.1525   2.730  0.00634 **
turnovers        0.1401     0.1830   0.765  0.44401   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 37.628  on 34  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 18.536  on 32  degrees of freedom
AIC: 24.536

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6

tidy(win_model_multi)

# A tibble: 3 × 5

The simplest model — team_winner ~ field_goal_pct — remains the best-supported one. field_goal_pct coefficient = 0.3977, p = 0.0055 — statistically significant. Higher field goal percentage is strongly associated with a higher chance of winning.
Odds ratio = 1.488 — each additional percentage point of FG% multiplies Michigan State’s odds of winning by about 1.49 (a ~49% increase in odds per point).
Intercept = -16.52 (odds ≈ 6.67e-08) — essentially, a team shooting 0% FG has near-zero odds of winning. Sanity check passes, but not meaningful on its own since FG% = 0 never actually happens.
Deviance drop: null deviance 37.628 → residual deviance 19.120, a big reduction, meaning FG% is doing real explanatory work. AIC = 23.12.

I want to see if there is a relationship between turnovers MIchigan State commits during a game and the amount of points the turnovers caused.

  geom_jitter(size = 5) +

Interpretation
What this means for your analysis: it explains why turnovers came back statistically insignificant (p = 0.444) in your two-predictor logistic model. The model wasn’t wrong or broken — the underlying data genuinely doesn’t show a strong relationship here. This is actually a good, honest finding for your report: despite the intuitive assumption that turnovers should heavily influence winning, the data says field goal percentage is doing essentially all the work, and turnovers aren’t adding meaningful predictive power on top of it.

If turnovers really mattered a lot, you’d expect losses (green) to cluster in one area — say, high turnovers/low turnover-points — and wins (orange) to cluster somewhere clearly different, the way wins and losses separated cleanly by field goal percentage in your earlier plots.
Instead, green and orange dots are scattered all over the same space. Michigan State has won games with very high turnover-point totals (~29-31) and also lost games with very few turnovers (~4.5). There’s no visible zone that’s “loss territory” versus “win territory.”
Logistic regression model- probability of winning by field goal pct

Both predictor variables(field goal percentage and turnovers have a positive coefficient, which inplies that both of the predictor variables do influence winning in a positive way. title: Chiefs 2024: A Season Built on Close Wins
text: The 2024 Kansas City Chiefs didn’t blow teams off the field — they beat them late, over and over. Across 17 games, Kansas City went 15-2 (88.2% win rate), and the story of that record is written in the margins: 5 of their 15 wins came by three points or fewer.

title: Chiefs 2024: A Season Built on Close Wins
text:
Week-by-Week Scoring


Show Code
ggplot(kc, aes(x = week)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = team_score), color = "#E31837", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = opp_score), color = "#666666", linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(aes(y = team_score, color = result), size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Win" = "#E31837", "Loss" = "#1a1a1a"), name = "Result") +
  labs(
    title    = "Kansas City Chiefs — Points Scored by Week (2024)",
    subtitle = "Red = points scored | Gray dashed = points allowed",
    x = "Week", y = "Points"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









Nothing about that chart looks dominant. The Chiefs rarely pulled away — their average scoring margin for the season was just +3.5 points per game, thin for a team that finished 15-2.

title: Chiefs 2024: A Season Built on Close Wins
text:
Living on the Margin


Show Code
ggplot(kc, aes(x = factor(week), y = margin, fill = close_game)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#FFB81C", "FALSE" = "#E31837"),
                     labels = c("TRUE" = "Decided by ≤ 3", "FALSE" = "Other"),
                     name = NULL) +
  labs(
    title = "Margin of Victory / Defeat by Week",
    x = "Week", y = "Point Margin (Team − Opponent)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")









Five of Kansas City’s wins were decided by three points or fewer — a Week 2 escape against Cincinnati (26-25), a two-point win over Denver in Week 10, a three-point win over Carolina, and two-point wins over Las Vegas and the Chargers in consecutive weeks (13 and 14). That’s a full quarter of the season’s wins coming down to the final possession.

Kansas City played it close no matter where the game was — the road record held up nearly as well as the record at home, a sign of a team that didn’t lean on a friendly building to win football games.

title: Chiefs 2024: A Season Built on Close Wins
section: The Best and Worst of It
text:
The Best and Worst of It
The best result of the year came against PIT in Week 17, a 29-10 win (+19). The worst came in Week 18 against DEN, a 0-38 blowout loss — a Week 18 finale that looks far more like a team resting starters with the top seed already locked up than a real measuring stick.

title: Chiefs 2024: A Season Built on Close Wins
text:
Takeaway
Strip out that meaningless Week 18 finale and Kansas City finished the meaningful part of its schedule 15-1, and did it the hard way — five wins by a field goal or less, a road record nearly as good as its home record, and no game all season that looked like a laugher in their favor. Teams that win close games all year either have something real (a quarterback who doesn’t blink, a defense that gets one stop when it has to) or they’re due for regression. The 2024 Chiefs bet on the former, and for one more year, it held up.

I would like to create a simple logistic regression model for Michigan State basketball.
A single numeric predictor- Is there a meaningful way to predict whether Michigan State basketball wins or not? The outcome variable is team_winner(win/loss) and the predictor variable is field goal percentage.
Interpretation of logistic model
The S-curve is doing exactly what it should. Below about FG% = 35, the curve sits near 0 — Michigan State essentially never wins games where they shoot that poorly. Above about FG% = 55, the curve is pinned near 1 — they essentially always win. In between, the curve rises steeply, which visually confirms the strong effect size your model found (odds ratio ≈ 1.49 per point of FG%).


Logistic regression model. A simple model with one numeric variable as a predictor variable

summary(win_model)

    data = msu_basketball)

Coefficients:

field_goal_pct   0.3977     0.1433   2.776  0.00550 **

Residual deviance: 19.120  on 33  degrees of freedom
AIC: 23.12

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6




Interpretation of a generalized logistic model.
The natural logarithm of the odds is (0.3977) which is positive so the probability is increasing. It tells us the direction of travel. As field_goal_percentage increases the chances of winning increase, due to the positive score. The p value is (0.00550) which is very small. This is a statistically significant result. This model tell us that field goal percentage has a positive relationship with winning games.
Because team_winner is binary, this model estimates the log-odds of winning as a function of field goal percentage — a positive field_goal_pct coefficient means higher shooting percentages are associated with a higher probability of winning. Exponentiating the coefficient (exp(coef(win_model))) converts that into an odds ratio: for example, a value of 1.15 would mean each one-point increase in field goal percentage multiplies the odds of winning by 1.15, holding nothing else constant.
Bottom line for your write-up: there is a statistically significant, strong positive relationship between field goal percentage and win probability for Michigan State this season — each additional point of FG% is associated with roughly a 49% increase in the odds of winning.
Field goal percentage matters, and matters a lot statistically. The coefficient for field_goal_pct is 0.3977 with p = 0.0055 — well under the standard 0.05 threshold, so there’s strong evidence of a real relationship, not just noise in this sample.
Odds ratio (that last output, exp(coef)): 1.488458 for field_goal_pct. That means for every 1-percentage-point increase in field goal percentage, the odds of Michigan State winning multiply by about 1.49 — roughly a 49% increase in the odds of winning per point of FG%. That compounds fast: a 10-point jump in FG% would multiply the odds by 1.49^10 ≈ 57x.

    Null deviance: 37.628  on 34  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 18.536  on 32  degrees of freedom
AIC: 24.536

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6




Interpretation
Both predictor variables(field goal percentage and turnovers have a positive coefficient, which inplies that both of the predictor variables do influence winning in a positive way. The p value is low for field goal percentage which inplies that it is statistically significant, however the p value for turnovers is a big high, which tell us that turnovers do not influence whether or not the teams wins as much as field goal percentage does. Turnovers is not a good candidate to include in the model because of the high p value. The AIC in this model is 24.53. The AIC in the single numeric variable is 23.12. The AIC is increasing which is not a good sign for an improved model. The Residual deviance has decreased from 19.120 to 18.536 which is a good sign. The residual deviance measures how well the model fits the data. It is a summary of the discrepencies between observed outcomes and the models predictions. Lower values indicate a better fit.

field_goal_pct is a significant predictor of winning (p = 0.006) — each 1-point increase in FG% (as a proportion, so realistically a 0.01 unit change) raises the log-odds of winning by 0.42.
turnovers is not significant (p = 0.444) — with only 35 games, there’s not enough evidence turnovers move the needle once FG% is in the model.

  geom_smooth(method = lm) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Field goal percentage and turnover levels",
       x = "Field goal percentage",
       y = "turnovers")
